title:   1. There is always more than one side to anything that exists. Look for both sides.   Ask Wise Mind: What am I missing? Where is the kernel of truth in the other side?

pages.text:
151
From DBT Skills Training Handouts and Worksheets, Second Edition by Marsha M. Linehan. Copyright 2015 by Marsha M. Linehan. Permission
to photocopy this handout is granted to purchasers of this book for personal use only (see copyright page for details).
Interpersonal Effectiveness Handout 16   (I. E. Worksheets 11–11b)
How to Think and Act Dialectically

 1.	There is always more than one side to anything that exists. Look for both sides.

 Ask Wise Mind: What am I missing? Where is the kernel of truth in the other side?

 Let go of extremes: Change “either-or” to “both-and,” “always” or “never” to
“sometimes.”

 Balance opposites: Validate both sides when you disagree, accept reality, and work to
change.

 Make lemonade out of lemons.

 Embrace confusion: Enter the paradox of yes and no, or true and not true.

 Play devil’s advocate: Argue each side of your own position with equal passion.

 Use metaphors and storytelling to unstick and free the mind.

 Other ways to see all sides of a situation:

 2.	Be aware that you are connected.

 Treat others as you want them to treat you.

 Look for similarities among people instead of differences.

 Notice the physical connections among all things.

 Other ways to stay aware of connections:

 3.	Embrace change.

 Throw yourself into change: Allow it. Embrace it.

 Practice radical acceptance of change when rules, circumstances, people, and
relationships change in ways you don’t like.

 Practice getting used to change: Make small changes to practice this (e.g., purposely
change where you sit, who you talk with, what route you take when going to a familiar
place).

 Other ways to embrace change:

 4.	Change is transactional: Remember that you affect your environment and your
environment affects you.

 Pay attention to your effect on others and how they affect you.

 Practice letting go of blame by looking for how your own and others’ behaviors are
caused by many interactions over time.

 Remind yourself that all things, including all behaviors, are caused.

 Other ways to see transactions:
Note. Adapted from Miller, A. L., Rathus, J. H., & Linehan, M. M. (2007). Dialectical behavior therapy with suicidal adolescents. New York:
Guilford Press. Copyright 2007 by The Guilford Press. Adapted by permission.

ourspace_shared_life_filter.title:   1. There is always more than one side to anything that exists. Look for both sides.   Ask Wise Mind: What am I missing? Where is the kernel of truth in the other side?
ourspace_shared_life_filter.shared_facing_summary: Use this to protect both people: needs are real, boundaries are kind, and a pause is not abandonment when repair is named.
tiny_steps: Name the ask in one sentence.
Agree on a return time or next check-in.

flare_day_versions.minimum: One breath, one label, one safe message, or one checkbox.
flare_day_versions.medium: Two to five minutes with everything reachable from bed/chair.
flare_day_versions.full: Use the original worksheet/handout when both bodies and brains have capacity.

accessibility_modifications: Use text/chat if speech is hard.
Include caregiver capacity as part of the plan, not an afterthought.
relationship_and_caregiver_notes: William's needs and Jasper's capacity both count.
Ask before helping; ask before escalating; name the smallest next step.
Pause means pause, not abandonment. I am coming back. I care about us. I need a smaller next step.
What is needed, what capacity exists, what can be smaller, and what can wait?
identity_and_trauma_filter: Use William's masculine language and Jasper's they/them language.
Use consent-based, non-shaming, body-neutral language unless a person has explicitly chosen a term.
Do not turn symptoms, dysphoria, trauma responses, clutter, fatigue, or emotional intensity into moral failure.
avoid_or_adjust: Do not use skills to win, corner, threaten leaving, or make either person prove pain or disability.
